I want to welcome everybody, good group today. Writing proposals is kind of an interesting proposition. You know, for those of us who are writers or marketers, we tend to do a lot of writing to prospects and prospects tend to be kind of gray area. You know, we're all used to writing to prospects that we don't see, we don't know them. And we kind of, we have samples of other writers' work that we can emulate or at least learn from. And so, you know, like that's one thing. But then when it comes to writing our proposal, a lot of us tend to freeze. We think, oh my goodness, this is a whole different animal. You know, I've got to really kind of overdo this thing and, you know, go into unknown kind of forbidden territory with it. And the opposite is actually true. I've been on both the receiving end of many, many proposals as well as having written many for my clients over the years. And the fact of the matter is that pretty much every dollar I've earned since early 90s can be traced to some kind of proposal that I made to a client. And in a very real way, writing these proposals is the most important sales copy you'll ever write because they structure the deal that then allows you to go on and make tons of money with a particular client if your proposal and the resulting contract is favorable to both of you. Having said all that, one of the things I've been thinking about is that the best proposal is the one you never have to write. It's the one that just basically is a verbal thing. Hey, why don't I do this and you pay me that and you're working with a client that you respect and trust. You have a history together. He says, yes, you go to work and you collect the money. Simple, right? The second best proposal is one that you can write on the back of a cocktail napkin. Some of the greatest breakthroughs in history have been written on the back of a cocktail napkin. I know, for example, Art Laffer who originated the Laffer Curve, one of the key points of supply-side economics demonstrated it on the back of a cocktail napkin. In fact, he did it several times and now those napkins go for fairly good prices among people who appreciate economics. But simplicity is the key to a great proposal. And beyond that, it should be part of a natural process. Several years ago, I had been working freelance for WICE research. I'd been writing, I wrote a promotion for Safe Money Report, then I wrote a promotion for another newsletter they had at the time called the Y2K Alert. They were gangbusters promotions. They brought in tons of new subscribers that break even or better. And for the first time in a long time, Martin's list was beginning to grow. And more importantly, during that process, Martin and I have become buddies. We talked about this before that one of the most important things, one of the most important components of any partnership is chemistry between you and the business owner you're working with. And Martin and I had it. We're both very difficult people. We're both very quirky. Martin is very left brain, detail oriented and tends to begin approaching every idea from a negative viewpoint. So he shoots holes in them. I tend to be more right brain and I tend to be more optimistic. So I see every idea is a great idea. And I'm never 100% right and he's never 100% right. So we balance each other. And that chemistry has worked well for us and learning to appreciate that and really love that in each other. It has been really the linchpin of our relationship. So I'm not just kind of throwing that out there. That is a key in choosing the clients you're going to work for. Not only do you feel that you, or not only have you proven, or do you feel that you could create winners for him and he agrees, but also that you enjoy working together and you trust each other. So anyway, we took our wives out of Caribbean cruise together, a little five day cruise, just to relax and we thought it might be a fun time. We took our two young children with us. And I was still working for his competitors. I was still doing a lot of work in the health field as well as the financial field. And I had no agenda. My goal in life there was to go hang out, eat some good food, see some sights, have a good time. But I could tell that Martin wasn't relaxed. He did have an agenda. And he just kept saying things that made it clear to me. He was kind of poking around, trying to figure out a way to deepen the work relationship and get more out of me. And finally one morning, about two or three mornings into the cruise, we were both real early risers. So, you know, we both get up around three, four in the morning. So we're sitting out on deck just chatting. And he made a couple of comments about how, you know, we've made such a good start and that there was so much potential. And I just looked at him. I said, do you want to take this thing to the moon? You know, do you want to just focus all our energies on it and ride this horse until it won't ride anymore? And he said, he got all excited. Absolutely. So the next couple of days, we spent a couple hours every day just kind of chatting. We were kind of chatting about what was needed in this company. You know, first, what the opportunities were. Second, what the challenges were, why he wasn't taking advantage of those opportunities. We talked about the strengths and weaknesses in his company in terms of personnel and infrastructure. We talked about his management style and how it was, in some cases, really helping the company, but also in some cases, it was holding us back. And we talked about, you know, basically the whole scope of it. And by the time we were done there, I was able to go back, crank out a one-page letter and say, okay, here's what we're going to do. Here's what you're going to pay me. And within a couple of weeks, we had a contract and within a month, I was drawing very nice royalty checks from Martin. So, you know, you're not going to be able to emulate that kind of situation in every case, but if you kind of take that as the ideal and kind of think about what happened there, I think it's going to help you in writing your proposals. Because one of the major errors that people make is that they jump into writing the proposal too soon. Okay? A client may be, you know, you may say something to a client like, well, you know, you need a direct mail piece to promote this or, you know, we need to form a partnership or, you know, whatever the idea is, and the client says, okay, give me a proposal. Well, what do you do now? You know, how far do you go with this proposal? Do you have to sell him on the idea of working together? Do you have to sell him on every idea you could possibly come up with? And how are you going to do that before you really know, first of all, what his vision is, what his company's strengths and weaknesses are, and all the other things? And so, you know, the proposal itself should really be kind of at the middle, almost a tipping point of the process that takes you from the handshake or the idea of a deeper relationship all the way through to the money on the other side. And the process really is, you know, first you need to talk with the decision maker and you need to know what his vision is, what his frustrations are, what his fears are, what he's eager to get done, what he, you know, would love to have someone else do that he doesn't have to mess with. Okay. And some other things we'll look at some of the questions that need to be asked in that all-important interview. Secondly, and this is something I haven't included in what we're going to do today, but I think it's really important to visit the company and to learn from the staff, because the fact of the matter is, and I run a company so I know this is true, the person at the top only thinks he knows what's really going on. He has a view of how his staff interacts, how things happen, who is competent and who is this, that's partial at best, and certainly when it comes to the problems. And so getting involved with the staff, just taking a day and asking to meet all of his key managers and spend a little bit of time with each one of them and find out what their vision is, whether it's consistent with the owner's vision, what problems they see, what are their frustrations. You're essentially asking them, what can I do for you? What is it that you want? Okay. Then the third step is to go to the web and research his competitors and also get a full understanding of what your target company is actually doing. And that's an excellent opportunity to pick up new ideas that you may or may not otherwise have come up with. So now at this point, you know where the decision maker is, you know where the staff is, you know where the competition is, and you have your own set of ideas for what you want to do with this company. And so now it's time to write the proposal. And just as a minor point, but maybe not so minor, the word proposal is a little bit intimidating or can be, especially to underlings at a company that you want to work with. I like to call my proposals discussion documents. By calling it a discussion document, you're making it clear this isn't what we're going to do. These are my ideas and I want your input. Now the staff feels like they're part of the team. They're not being shunted aside by this expert from out of town with a briefcase. They're much less to be intimidated. And that means, by the way, that they're much less to be basically nailing you to a cross every time you leave the room, which is one of the things that happens quite often when you're working, when you're an outsider working with a company. So then from that proposal or discussion document, the next step, which is made clear from the outset, is a discussion to revise and finalize the proposal, which in essence is a plan of where you're going to go from now. Then the proposal should set up the contract and make negotiating the contract a no-brainer. And then the next step is that you start cash-in checks from the work you've done. So let's take a look at some of the steps that I think about when I'm creating a proposal or a discussion document and how I kind of move through the process linearly. Now, it's important to understand that not all of these steps, and I think it was Cheryl, I think it was who wrote to me and said that she's in the middle of preparing a proposal right now. It's a different kind of proposal. She's actually been retained by her client to write a proposal for the client to the government, and they're not selling a product, they're selling services. So some of the things that will cut, some of the broader principles will cover, I think will come in very handy for you. However, you're going to have to think your way through of how that kind of proposal differs from the partnering proposals that we're actually talking about today. One of the things that I use most often around here is ready, fire, aim. So often we're in such a hurry to get things done that we don't really stand back from a project and say, hey, wait, what is our ultimate objective here? What effect do we want this document to have on the person who's going to read it? So before you begin writing any proposal, it's important to understand what your aims are. And when I'm creating a proposal, for example, the first thing that I want to do with a proposal is to confirm that my mission is to fulfill my client's fondest business desires. I want to address the emotions that he has right now about his business, that either he is enjoying the fantasy of explosive growth or that he would rather get rid of like his frustrations and his fears. And so that's part of this initial interview with the client. And we talked about also going to the company and asking these same questions of the managers so that they're more likely to be on your side as they work through your finished proposal. Then you want to add something new, okay? For several reasons. You want to come in with new ideas, things they haven't thought of, or better ways to do things that they're contemplating now. We were approached a few weeks ago by a company that's developing a software product, and they've been working on it for some time, and they had some very clear ideas of how they wanted this product to go, what they wanted it to do, how much they would charge for it, and how they would market it. But they contacted us because they wanted help, they wanted more ideas. We took a look at the product and we said, it's a great product. But there are four or five things about the way you're planning to develop the product that we think could be done better. And we think your pricing is absolutely wrong. And we were able to go through those things with this client. And that's the proposal I'll show you with any parts that need to be redacted as soon as it's complete. But by bringing those new things in, we made the pipe bigger. We were able to turn the one product into three products. We were able to turn a $5 product into a $3,500 product. We were able to give them ideas for positioning in the product that would make it palatable to a far larger market segment than they were originally contemplating being able to access. And so you want to add something new. The document then, by doing that, provides value, a reason why it should be read and thought about. But it also demonstrates your expertise as you do those things. However, you can imagine if you add something new without really understanding the current situation of the corporation and what the vision is, what the problems are, you're likely to end up sitting there with your face hanging out. While they tell you that, hey, we can't do that, we've done that before, miserable failure, a competitor did, he went out of business, or that's not the direction we want to go, or whatever their objection is. So by doing the interviews first, you'll know what the objections are. So even if you want to insist on an idea that you believe is right for the company, you'll at least be able to answer those objections in your proposal. And then finally, you want your proposal to include all the information an attorney would need to begin drawing up your contract. Now ideally, you want this to be a slippery slope for the client. You know, you want him to read the thing, love the ideas, get excited about your vision, be comfortable with your compensation package, and be able to just email your proposal to his attorney and say, draw up a contract. Okay, so, and I think we'll mention this as we get further into this, but there's a, I'll say it now and then I'll say it again later. One of the mistakes I made early on in my career was that I was timid about demand, about presenting my compensation package up front in the proposal. And that's a serious error. If you do everything else right, the client is counting the money and the benefit of having you aboard as you're presenting your ideas. Okay, and so, for the first part of the thing, he's going to be just thrilled. He's going to be all excited. But see, if you get down to the point of saying, okay, well, you're going to have to pay me every month and you're going to have to pay me this much in sales and these royalties, all this other stuff. And he freezes, you know, then you know right away, you've got a problem and you can either negotiate it or you can walk away and save a hell of a lot of time. If your compensation isn't in the initial proposal and you say, well, let's, you know, just talk about what we want to do together because you want to avoid conflict, you're just going to end up wasting time with a lot of the prospects you deal with. So make sure there's a complete document, something that you could just simply hand to an attorney and say, okay, drop a contract. Okay, so let's look at some of the questions that I like to ask business owners during that exploratory phase prior to beginning my proposal writing. I want to know what his mission statement is. This is not some kind of phony, baloney, bullshit business school thing. This is, you know, where is it you want to take your company? Why are you in this business? You know, and you want to hear something like, well, you know, we want to bring value to people's lives. We want to do it by making the lawn greener, making the waste thinner, putting more hair on their head or, you know, whatever it is a company does. And you want to hear that the owner has a passion for that. And you can draw him out, get stories as to why he's passionate about that. But, you know, this kind of thing is going to give you, you know, a very clear vision of what your client is ultimately going to ask you to do in the full spectrum of the kind of work you'll be doing for. Then I ask what's your short, medium and long term vision for your company? Now, this mission statement has more to do with what impact you want to have on the world. You know, Martin Weiss wants to help people protect their money in bad times and help them make money in good times. Health resources wants to help people live better and healthier, you know, without having to pay a fortune for doctors or drugs. Every company is driven by some passionate central desire. The next one, the vision for the company is how do you see the company growing? How would you like to see it growing? How quickly would you like to see it grow? How high do you want to create your own special niche? Do you want to be number one in existing niche or several of them? Which niches are those? You know, and when you're talking to the owner especially, you want to know what he sees his involvement being going forward. You know, one of the things I think of at the very beginning with every new client is, you know, how is this going to end? You know, I remember Layne Lowry when we first started talking at Health Resources. I asked him, I said, So what's your vision? You want to run this company for the rest of your life? You're a young man. That could happen. You could run this company for another 30 years, you know, or you want to build it up to $100 million in a couple of years and then sell it and go on and do something else. You know, what is it that you want to do? What challenges do you think have to be overcome to achieve your vision? This is where the business owner and in turn the different managers get to bitch. They get to tell you, oh man, we've been trying to do this for so long, you know, I can't find good employees or I can't find good mailing lists or I can't find good copy or I can't find, you know, good product developers or whatever the challenges are that have kept him from achieving his vision so far. What are his greatest frustrations in terms of achieving the vision? Which is another way of asking this, but this is, you want to dig a little deeper because ultimately guys, he's not going to sign your contract because it's logical to do so. He's going to tell himself that and it needs to be logical to do so, but ultimately what's going to get you the deal you're looking for is making the business owner see that you will be able to fulfill his most powerful positive desires and emotions and ease or get rid of the negative ones. So, you know, dig in deep there. What's your greatest fear? We all have them, you know. A business owner has to make payroll every single month. Even if he's got more money than got his cash reserves in that company, he watches like a hawk and anytime he goes through a month or a quarter where those start receding, that's a huge fear for him. Maybe his fear is a regulatory one. Maybe it's a litigation, fear of litigation that a customer might sue him. Find out what his greatest fear is because that'll be a key to a great working relationship and also a great proposal. Ask him why he thinks people buy his product. You know, sometimes I get some very funny answers to that question and sometimes they're very instructive. I've told this story before of how one company I work with, they sell a product, a three-dimensional product, right? But their customer service people are always available. If you have a question or you don't like the product, you want to return it, whatever the thing is, or you want to order more, you know, there's a toll-free number and people are encouraged by it. Well, his market is older and so he's finding that, you know, his customer service manager came to him and said, hey look, you know, I just started at your company, I'm from another company, we limited our customer service calls to three minutes each. Your customer service calls are an average of 15 minutes each. We need to let people know that when they call in, you know, they can't be telling us about their aches and pains and their daughter who just graduated college and all this other crap, they need to get their business done and get off the phone. And my client said, hey, you're nuts. What we're going to do is hire more customer service people because our people are older, they're bored. It's a high point in their day to be able to call us and talk to somebody who's friendly, you know, and who's interested in them. That's part of our product and so we're going to add that to it. So you'll get some interesting answers there. Again, what are the most common causes for customer complaints? This question helps you on two levels. Number one, I'll tell you what the owner and the managers think the most common cause is, but hopefully you'll also be able to dig deeper and find out what it actually is. And the two are quite different and quite often. And another thing that you need to look at is what's the frequency of customer complaints. You'd be surprised how many companies are being run on the basis of some underling walking into the boss's office and saying, we're getting a ton of complaints about that promotion we sent out yesterday. And the boss goes, oh no, that's terrible, a ton of complaints. Let's change the promotion or let's change the strategy or let's change the product or let's do this. And you'd be shocked to know how many business owners say, okay, well, out of our total list, what percent is that? Out of the total people who received that promotion, how many complaint? Okay, or out of the total number of people who bought that product, how many complaint? And so without putting in perspective, that statement from the person who's coming to the owner with a problem is meaningless and certainly shouldn't be acted upon. But most business owners don't see it as that. They don't have time to do the analysis. And so they take that kind of baloney at face value and in most cases it costs them money. So you want to know what the owner thinks the most common causes are, but you also want to know what the most common causes really are and what percentage, how high is his complaint volume on each of the top topics that people are complaining about. So you thought this was all going to be about sitting around and typing up a proposal. Like everything else in life, the actual writing of the proposal is less important than the thinking behind it. So you need also ask the owner who the strongest managers are, what the weakest areas are on his staff, what he's tried that didn't work, and why do you think it failed. And this can apply to marketing, but it can also apply to product development, corporate structure, promoting particular people to particular positions. What do you think have been your biggest failure running this company over the last couple of years? It's great intelligence to have and it will give you a lot of ideas that you can then pass on for helping the company grow. These are the two critical questions. What do you want from me? You don't have to ask it that way, but everything that you've asked so far has been leading up to this. What is it I can do for you? Well, I want you to build my list. I have 100,000 customers. I want 200,000 customers. I'm pulling in $100 a month from my average customer on the back end. I want to pull in $200 a month from my average customer on the back end. Or he may just say, well, I want to double sales. Or I want to cut my costs. Or I want to beat the living daylights out of a hated competitor. What do you want from me? Tell me what you want me to do. And then what are you willing to give me if I give you what you want? Again, this is not a yes question, but before you waste time writing a big proposal, I like to give some clear cues to what this is going to cost them. In one company that we're in negotiations with right now, I told them right up front, you know, if I'm writing a customer acquisition program for the mail, you pay me $50 a thousand. If I'm writing one for the web, you pay me $5 to $6 per lit. If I'm writing a promotion to sell something to the house file, you're going to pay me 10 to 20% on sales. And if I'm getting involved with the organization or company with staffing, with procedures, and with the other things that really make, you know, turn a Model T company into a Bugatti marketing machine, then I want 10 to 20% of your stock. And if the client looks at me and smiles and says, not a single problem in there, then I know that the deal is basically done. I just have some paperwork to do. If he blanches, then I want to know right up front what his objections are, what his concerns are. I had a guy the other day I was talking to, not too long ago, actually, well, I guess it was two months ago. And his concern, he had two concerns. One was litigation, the other one was cash management. He's contemplating turning the marketing over to us, period, you know, and those were his concerns. Okay, he was happy with the money I was thinking about, right? And he was happy with, he knew, well, we've worked together before, he knew what I could provide. But he had those two concerns. And knowing that early on, really helped me with the proposal. We're getting some questions. Let me address them here. Cheryl says, I had a history with Martin, but what about new clients? Are they triggering events, things going on in the news, or economy or specific industry initiatives? Do you recommend that we pay attention to or look for? Good question, Cheryl. A couple of things. First of all, yes, I had a history with Martin, but a big part of what I've been saying so far is that you need to develop some kind of history with your prospective client prior to spending the time and effort of trying to put together a long-term relationship. And that, now that experience could be as simple as, as I just mentioned, spending time with him and, or his company, and really getting to know them. And making sure that you make plenty of time for social time so that you can get to know each other on a social level as well as in the office. Because, you know, first of all, there's bonding involved there that'll make things easier. And then secondly, you'll actually learn a lot. I've learned more about Martin's company and Sushi bars than I have in his office. So, the relationship is important and you can build a history even if it's not a work history. You know, there are situations where we're approached because reputation people have heard of us. And so they already know what we can provide and everything and they want to work with us. So we don't have a working relationship to start with. There are other times when you may approach a company that hasn't heard of you. But that is, then you have to begin building a relationship from scratch and you do that through the interview process, through visiting a company, and through making sure that the company has a really good view. The business owner has a really good view of what you bring to the table, what you've done before for other people and so forth. Okay, so that's the first part of your question. Second part of your question, if you're contemplating setting up a partnership agreement with a particular company in a given niche, market or niche, by all means you need to immerse yourself in everything about that niche. And when I say everything, I mean subscribe to the industry publications, watch the news, search the news online, you can go to Yahoo News, you can search for any news about pretty much any sector of the economy, find out what's going on in that sector. Is it experiencing problems? Is it expanding? Is it contracting? How are oil prices affecting it? How are other rising commodity prices affecting it? All of that will make you, when you sit down and talk to this company about where they're going, they don't want to talk to somebody who doesn't know their industry from a whole underground. I see this a lot in the investment field. Guys, if any of you want to write for investment companies, do what Haroon is doing right now. Haroon is one of my copywriters here at Response Bank. He gets up on his own volition, no one suggests that he do this. He gets up at 7 o'clock, this morning it was at 7.12 I hear, in order to attend a 7-15 conference call with Vice Research and I. On that call, we talk about what happened in the economy yesterday, what's happening on our list, what's happening with our business. Haroon is getting an air full of what the Fed's lack of action yesterday really means. What rising oil prices mean, what the softening demand in the US for energy means. He's gone to school on the economy and on the investment markets and he's doing everything he can to come up to speed. When he leaves me, like Newtson just did, and goes out and starts his own consulting practice or his own freelance practice or begins partnering with companies, he has a financial client and the financial client says, Well, we're really worried about CDOs or derivatives or some exotic options. Haroon will know exactly what they're talking about. When the client says something like, the Fed is loaning hundreds of billions of dollars at the discount window, Haroon will know that that's inflationary and he'll know why that's a bad thing. So, know the industry. There's so many times when Martin and my other financial clients have hired great writers who just don't understand the investment industry and the results have been pathetic. So, yeah, that's a good question, Sheryl, and it's a big part of preparing. And Larry Meekus, hi Larry. He asks, if someone pays me X dollars per lead, how do you verify how many leads he has coming in? Well, it's very simple. Whether you do the leads on your own system, people opt in, for example, let's say it's an internet campaign which most people are doing these days. If you're doing it on your own system, you absolutely know how many are coming in. If the people opt in and the name goes directly to the company, then the company will know. But as part of your working agreement with the company, you need to have access to their system so that you can watch those leads as they come in. Also, there's another very important thing here. The whole thing of direct marketing is monitoring results. I spent $10,000 on paper quick this month. How many leads did I get? What was my cost per lead? Excuse me. So, you can't do a job to the client unless you have those numbers. You need to be getting daily reports that say, I have a client now that's spending $350,000 a month on paper quick. Fine. I want to see how many leads are coming in on that. I want to see what his cost is per lead because we're writing the paper quick ads and we're writing the squeeze pages. We're writing the sales pages and the gauntlets that follow. For me to do my job, I have to have those numbers. So, it would be foolish for the client to hold out on me in that case. In direct mail, it's a little more difficult if the lead is coming back in over the telephone or if it's coming back in through the mail. So, bottom line is, look, no piece of paper is worth anything. No contract is worth anything unless a person who is signed is trustworthy. And if you suspect that you're being ripped or if you suspect that a client is untrustworthy, don't even bother signing a contract because the signature is worthless. In my contracts, I quite often will have auditing clauses that give me the right to audit the client at any time. So, if I see an anomaly, if it seems to me that the numbers I'm saying are suppressed or depressed in some way, I have the right to demand an audit. And I can go in and look at their source documents and the reports from their call center, the reports from the mail room. So, I've never exercised that because I never signed contracts with people I don't trust. Okay, now you've gathered all your information. You basically know what the client wants, what the client needs, what he expects from you and what he's willing to pay you. Now it's a matter of sitting down and putting it on paper in a way that's likely to give you a quick acceptance. First of all, think of your proposal as B2B sales copy. That's essentially what it is. But it's not B2B sales copy to a nameless, faceless prospect. You need to respect your client's time. Remember what I said, the best proposal is when you never write or that you can write on the back of a napkin. The simpler, the quicker you can communicate your ideas, the better. The more you let your eyes roll up in your head and just kind of blah, blah, blah, to give him a more substantial document, unless he's going to read it. Respect your client's intelligence to avoid hyperbole, avoid overstatement and exaggeration. Just tell us straight, just be straight with him. Be short, be straight. Don't forget, other people will read this. He's going to show this to everybody on his staff who's opinion he trusts. Or respects. Or everyone he feels he has to show it to because he's kind of playing a major expenditure with you. Or a relationship that may change the inner workings of his company to some degree. So other people will read this. If you've done this right, you're sensitive to where the kingdoms are within his empire. You know who the strong people are, who the paranoid people are, who the insecure people are, who the real performers are. And keep that in mind as you're writing this. Do not include tactical things in proposals that will make any of the key decision makers in the company the slightest bit paranoid. Anticipate and answer questions and objections. You don't even have to raise the question or the objection. But if you know from talking to people, from doing your research, that there are going to be questions, oh boy Fred's going to object to this one. Then load that up with the reasons why. The reasons you would give Fred if you made that objection in person. If possible without actually raising the objection. And then provide some value but not too much. Don't feel like you have to lay out their entire marketing plan for the whole year. Don't feel like you have to teach them how to do marketing your way or show them how to do a break-even calculation or anything else. Provide value in the form of quick suggestions, contacts, tools. For example, you're writing copy and you know a great paper click guy. One of the things that you may mention in your proposal is that I'll put you in touch with my friend So-and-So who does a paper click work for IBM. The point of providing that some value in the contract is readership, just like it is in sales copy, readership and credibility demonstrates your expertise. But there's a danger in providing too much because you don't want them. The more detail that you give people about what you're going to do, the more possible objections you're going to get. I was involved in a call yesterday that drove me nuts. I finally just hung up in frustration. I had sent through five ideas to name an online event. There were, let's see, one, two, three, five people on the call. And immediately they started parsing each one of the words into each one of these titles and subtitles. Now at this group, if you gave them the title Gone with the Wind or Warrent Peace or the Holy Bible, they would tear the living crap out of it. And they would tell you all the reasons why that was the worst title ever written. Because of this word or that word or this nuance or that nuance. And frankly, if you left them in a room long enough with it, they'd end up with something that was so horrible it couldn't be used. So I finally just said, look, there are too many cooks spoiling this show, I'm out of here. And I just pick one, I don't care. And that was the end of that. Now that's what happens when you provide a proposal that has way too much detail in it. You're giving people little hooks that they can grab onto and extend this process. All the details can be worked out later. It's the working agreement and the general direction that you want to cover in your proposal. You want to sell the dream right up front. Now we're beginning to talk about the actual structure of the proposal. At the very beginning of the proposal, you want to sell the dream. You want to be specific about this is where you regurgitate what the owner's dream is. What he is, where he wants to take the company, what he wants to do in terms of the company's mission statement. All of the things that he told you he wants you to do for him in those interviews. Then take the dream farther. Give it some dimension. You're painting a painting here. Give it some dimension and think about some of the other things. A guy may tell you, look, my product is the best in the industry. We just don't get any recognition. What he's really saying is I want to be the president of our trade group. I need a little recognition here. Or he may be saying that. If you think he is saying that, then be thinking about, read between the lines and give him what he wants here. Draw the picture of where his company will be in three years. Then tell him how you're going to get there. This is basically how we say when you're writing copy, tell him what you're going to tell him. Then tell him what you told him. This is where you tell him what you're going to tell him. This is just a big broad stroke. What we're going to do is create a marketing group here. The marketing group is going to do this and that and the other thing. We're going to have campaigns in these media. Our goal is to bring in as many new customers, as many new leads every month, as many new customers per month. If you have other metrics like cost per sale or return on investment, this is the executive summary. This is particularly important. I think when your contract runs more than four or five pages long, if your proposal runs more than four or five pages long. The next step is list the challenges. We just looked at where we want to go. Here are the things that are holding us back. These are some of the things that you want to look at. The company, the spokesperson, and the product positioning. One of the things you'll run into most commonly is that the company is totally in love with a product, but they don't really have a spokesperson. People don't bond with products, they bond with people. Let's make the owner of the company a celebrity. Let's make him a spokesperson. If he doesn't want to do that, let's go hire one. Crynell Edless put a face on this product. How should the company be positioned? How should the product be positioned? I told you all the SRC stories, so I won't recount it. In that story, you saw how simply repositioning the product, not so much the company, but the product we're selling, rare coins, as not something for numismatic nerds, but rather something that had a memory, if this coin could talk, what would it say? It was a connection to the romance of the old west. Just making that change in positioning was huge. If the company's positioning could be improved, this is a place to talk about it. Product development. Chances are, in many cases, the product line is fixed, or at least static for the time being, and that's fine. But if there are ways to improve the product, this is a good place to talk about it. If you think the product is standing in the way of growth, it needs to be improved. The infrastructure of the company, what do they need in terms of facilities to create the change? What's holding them back? Do they need a television studio? Do they need CD duplicators? Do they need five new employees? Well, wait, we'll get to five new employees in a minute. What hardware, software, and other requirements do they need in order to build a foundation for the growth that you're about to create in the company? Organization and personnel. Part of your initial investigation of the company, you're looking at how the company's structured, how orders come down from the top, how input feedback comes back from the bottom. Unfortunately, the way corporations are structured is the customers at the bottom end. The marketing department is next, and then you've got this whole bunch of lords and ladies who are involved in bean counting and legal issues and all kinds of other personnel, all kinds of other crap, who never sold anything in their lives actually running things. The only input, the feedback that matters, which is coming from customers, is kind of filtering up to the marketing people, but they tend not to have a lot of authority in the company. And that's one thing that you're going to really want to change when you move into a company. You're going to want to turn that thing upside down so that the customers on top, the marketing people, lead the charge because the customers are telling them what they want. They're getting the data, and that the president, the bean counter, the lawyer, the personnel, people, everyone else in that company, their job is to help marketing do its job. You start your company that way, and you've got a winner on your hands. Okay, lead production and conversion, if that's something that is a challenge for them, you're going to have to solve that problem for them. But at any rate, in this part of the proposal, you would simply mention the fact that lead production is a problem. We've only produced this many leads, and they've been $10 a lead. What we need to do is develop online and offline lead production campaigns that enable us to bring in 10 times that number of leads at half the cost or whatever your metrics are. New customer acquisition is a problem for every company. This is the big one. You're going to find that they value this the most. New customer acquisition is the hardest thing to do. Customer sales to existing customers is the easiest thing. Some people make the mistake of saying, well, we're going to put our best people on new customer acquisition because that's where we have our capital risk. It costs us $500 a thousand to put promotion in the mail, or it costs us $300,000 to produce an infomercial. So we've got to have our best people there, and then we'll handle the house file ourselves. To me, that's kind of wrong. You want the absolute best you can get in both places. That means that you as a consultant, copywriter, rainmaker, marketing guy, need to be involved in both. New customer acquisition is a big deal because it represents part of the growth equation. Growth is number of customers times sales. This is part of the growth equation. The other part of it is how much can we sell to our existing customers? How much can we keep on board with us? You need to be involved in all three, customer acquisition, customer promotions, and retention. Here's where you outline the challenges in terms of customer acquisition. We've been able to find mailing lists that have performed better than a certain percent. Our SEO traffic really sucks. Our pay per click is costing us too much. We've budgeted a million dollars for pay per click, and we can't spend more than $100,000 and still keep our lead cost where it needs to be. Whatever the problems are that you've encountered, that's where this goes. Customer service. One of the first things that you do when you're dealing with a company is sit down all by yourself and figure out how to catch their customer service department really screwing up. This is fun. I really enjoy doing this. You just figure out the nightmare scenario for a customer service person. Put them through their paces. I think I told you that years ago when I first started working with Weiss, I called up their customer service people. I said, hey, look, I just got a promotion for this safe money report thing. It looks kind of interesting. I was wondering, tell me about what I get with it. They haltingly told me about the premiums and how often I got the newsletter. Nothing about what it would do for me, just the features stuff, the physical stuff. I said, okay, well, how much is it? They said, well, it's $99 a year. They didn't tell me the nominal price was $189, and this is a discount. That's it. What if I wanted two years? If you take two years, you get five more reports and it's $189 for two years. This sounds pretty good. I think I'll take three years. My anecdote is, I'm sorry, you can't buy a three-year subscription. Quick email to the owner of the company saying, okay, well, here's five quick ways that you can put money on the bottom line right now. But scripture customer service people, when people call in and they start asking questions about the product, have them scripted to present the benefits of the product, the guarantee, the offer, and for God's sake, to never say we won't take your order if you want three years. It makes you a hero, but it's also a real quick and easy way to put money on the bottom line. And by the way, it's also a way of demonstrating value because you didn't charge for that. You just did it. But anyway, in your document here, you'll want to have done that research and have, and of course, talk to the customer service people and address those challenges. Retention, make sure you've taken a look at their renewal rates or their reorder rates and outline the challenges that they're facing there. And then finally, public relations. A lot of people forget about this, especially people involved in direct response marketing. But I place high value on public relations. If you go to Martin's website, for example, Martin Weiss' website, and look at all of the media appearances that his people are making around the world, Larry Ellison, who's a really good buddy of mine, he hosts CNBC Asia over there. I mean, that's how good their public relations department is. And every time any one of their guys publishes a book, of course, they're all over the newspapers and all over TV. And I gotta tell you, it helps response. So you get more name recognition every time you get a full page ad on one of your clients in USA Today or the Wall Street Journal or someplace. And you're in the mail, you're gonna see a spike in response. So don't forget the public relations possibilities. Then outline your ideas for the product. Now, maybe it's perfect, I doubt it. Look for opportunities to improve the packaging, the quality of the product, what it does is functionality, its durability, those kinds of things. Look at the price and see if there are things that can be done there. I mean, ask the question, you're not a buyer's rep, but ask the question, what if we bought it in Mexico, what if we bought it in China? How are competitors getting the best possible costs at each step of the process for them? Maybe we should test a higher price, maybe we should test a lower price. Maybe we should gang products together and increase our average sale. So look at his pricing, look at his guarantees. Remember the principle, the longer the guarantee, the fewer refunds you'll get. Best guarantee I ever saw was on a package that I wrote for Rodel. It was actually their idea. And it was a lifetime heirloom guarantee by this book. You or your heirs can return it anytime with no time limits and get your 29 bucks back. Okay, but what does that do? I mean, now contrast that with, okay, you have a two-week guarantee. Take the book, review it for two weeks, you don't like it, send it back, and we'll refund your money. When you say two weeks, everybody's got a calendar on their refrigerator, they put a big red X on that date two weeks from now, and they write on that calendar, return Rodel book, you know? So they know they have that much time to read it, and then they'll return it. But if the deadline is way off in the distance, they're not, they're going to be less likely, text size guarantee. And so take a look at that guarantee. So often, business owners go for the shorter guarantee because it scares them to have a longer guarantee. But that's really a better way to go. You may run into some other issues with that that will become evident as you learn more about the industry that you're working in and how they do their accounting, but we'll leave that for another time. And look at the premiums. You know, what else do they get with a product? You know, let's say you're not selling a lawnmower, you know, you're not selling an investment newsletter or health supplements or, you know, any of the stuff that I deal with. Let's say you're selling a lawnmower, you know, what can you add to the package that the customer gets that brings additional value to him? Okay. Also, think about, is a great place, and a lawnmower is a good example. Let's say you're toro and you make lawnmowers, right? Well, you know, what does your guy want? Your customer really wants, does he want a lawnmower? Hell no. He doesn't want a lawnmower. Haroon's getting it. Yeah, he wants a coupon for a free fill-up, a free five-gallon fill-up, or he wants packets of flower seeds from another company that markets those. Or maybe he wants, you know, a free quart of oil for it, or he wants a video or a book on how to have the greenest lawn in the neighborhood, or on how to make his, you know, yard work go, you know, faster and easier. So, you know, think about, not just, you know, how can we chuck this up with premiums, but how can we really bring value to people's lives and make that price seem like even more of a bargain? Okay. I'd like your ideas for developing new products as well at this point. However, I wouldn't get into too much detail unless there's one or two of them that you're recommending that they begin on immediately. And then also your ideas for acquiring third-party products. This is, you've got a database. If the client has a product development problem, he can't develop as many products as his people would buy from him if he, you know, offered them the opportunity. Then acquiring third-party products is a way to go. Now, we've done that. And the cool thing here is it doesn't always have to be the, you know, the same kind of product client normally sells. I've sold water filters to investors, you know, water distillers. A friend of mine, I met him through, and doing this years ago, he attended my power market, marketing summit. His name was Jack. He imports and markets the water-wise water distiller, which is the best water distiller in the world. And so we introduced that to our file of back in the day, probably 150,000 to 200,000 investors. They sold like hotcakes. So, you know, don't think about your client's customers monolithically. They're not just because they bought a lawn mower doesn't mean that's the only thing that they buy. They're buying all kinds of stuff. And so going to them, sure, if you stay in the niche where they, where you have credibility, you know, you're going to do very well with it. But test products from other niches as well. If the guy's got a lawn, what does that tell you? He's probably a homeowner, right? He's got a car. He wants a house to look nice. He wants a car to look nice. What other products could you offer to that guy? If you're client sitting there with his file and not promoting it because he doesn't have more products to sell these people, that idea alone can put millions dollars on his bottom line. The next part of your proposal should discuss the market. And this is where his ideal prospects are hiding. Okay, and look at whose best customers are now in terms of frequency, average sale, total purchases per year, that kind of thing, and figure out where they came from in the first place and what their psychographics are. You know, are psychographics, what are their passions? One of the things I see happening a lot online right now, and this kind of ties into what I just said, is that if you subscribe to Newsmax Human Events, Drudge Report, or any of the other conservative news organizations, you're going to suddenly begin getting a lot of promotions for supplement, nutritional supplement products. Okay? So, you know, understand it now if you're a supplement company and you start looking at the psychographics of your file, and the sources where you've gotten your names, and you find out that you're getting a lot of names from people who are politically conservative, that gives you a lot of clues where to go find a hell of a lot more people who are just like your best customers now. So, in this market segment, this is where I will tend to outline the list and other, and we'll get to media in a moment, but, you know, this has more to do with psychography, it has to do with, wow, well, conservatives really like this product, or liberals really like this product, or little old ladies who subscribe to knitting magazines really like this product. So, this is where you get some clues as to where you should be going next to find another million or two customers just like the 100,000 they may have now. This is important to do anyway for, in terms of creating a marketing plan later on, which is something hopefully we'll be able to get to at some point. Next is a media, okay, you know who you're looking for, now the question is what's the most cost-efficient way to get to them. And so, in this section I would outline, chances are any company you're working with is going to have one, possibly two media that they're already using, chances are they'll have one that they're really doing well, really exploiting fully and really understand, and then another one or two they're dabbling in, but there's huge room for improvement here. So, in this section you need to go through and look at all of the available media for that client. And when I say available media, I'm not talking about, in most cases, I'm not talking about, well, geez, you know, the local newspapers and media. I'm talking about psychographically correct media for that client company. Okay, so, you know, on the web for example, all right, there are email lists available that you can rent. I hear they don't always work that well, some of them have worked okay for us, but it's not a prime way to drive traffic. Affiliate campaigns, now we're talking. If you have an investment product, you can go to every other investment, guy online who has a list and say, hey, let's all make some money together. You know, here's a product, you sell it to your file, we're doing that right now. Here's this product we created, this whole promotional campaign, it's all right done. Just send it to your file, keep half the money. Easing sponsorships, this is paid, you know, you pay for the space and somebody else's easing. And again, this is an area where if you're in the, you know, if you sell a product for people who like to make model boats, you know, you go find the easings that go to those people and you sponsor those easings, so they're psychographically correct. Same thing with banners, you go to sites that are, that make sense. You go to social networking sites, you work that, the joint ventures, indirect mail. You look at direct mail and all the list of people who bought products like yours before. In print ads, you look at all of the magazines and newspapers that go to people who are like the prospects that you're looking for. Typically people who, you know, rather magazines and newspapers whose subject matter is focused on the thing that you do. TV infomercials and or spots, again, the media here that's available is very broad. If you're selling a weight loss product, you probably do okay on pretty much any channel, but there are also health channels now. So, you know, beginning with the channels that are most psychographically correct and the same thing with radio infomercials. And then anything else that makes sense to you based on what the client's selling and who his prospects are. The next section is the strategy. We're going to have to start moving a little more quickly here. The next one is the strategy. Let's get back to it. At this point you've identified all kinds of opportunities. Okay, you've identified the products you want to sell, the media, who you're going to sell it to. You've looked at offers, you've looked at all of these other wonderful things, right? You have a lot of opportunities before you. Now it's important to list the opportunities that will produce the greatest benefit in the shortest amount of time at the lowest cost. As the rainmaker you want to go in and you want your first 90 days to be so memorable with that client that he would rather cut off his right arm than lose you. The way to do that is find those opportunities where you can produce a huge result quickly and do those first. So list the opportunities in order of how quickly you can get them done and what the return would be. Include a rough timeline for each project. Don't get down to first draft copies due now and second draft here and final draft here and legal compliance will take place on such and such a date. Just say that in the first six weeks we want to do these three campaigns. Just give rough timelines. Everyone understands that timelines change. Some of these things may be a hero and get them done sooner in some cases that may get pushed back. But remember when you're working with a client there's an awful lot of people in that mix and when things get held back it's usually not going to be your fault. Include a cost estimate and a target return for each. This is where you're selling the dream. My idea for you is we're going to take this product to a thousand affiliates in the first 30 days. They're going to sign up for a program. Together they have 10 million customers. They're going to recommend your free report to their 10 million customers. We anticipate that 1 to 2% of those people are going to download this free report. And we anticipate that 5 to 6% of those people are going to actually buy your product. That will give you revenue of this much. Your cost is that much. Which means you're going to clear this much. And the entire campaign will be over and done with by September 30th. So now the guy is starting to see the money pile up. He's starting to see you doing the work, making it happen. He's getting excited. And you need to do this for the immediate opportunities that you have. The next thing is the relationship. Specify the work that you'll do for the client. Cover all the bases. You're going to give them marketing strategy, testing strategy, copywriting, graphics direction, product ideas and development. General consulting. You're going to brainstorm with him on all of the above. You're going to give him advice on his organization, his processes, recruitment, resources. You're not going to let him make a mistake. You're going to be there for him. Basically this is where you tell him, you regurgitate his answer to what do you want me to do for you. What do you want from me? Then there's more to be done here. You need to specify how the work will be performed. Now this is really important. Whenever a company begins working with an outsider, there are a couple of concerns that they have. The first concern is that you will do something or say something that will reflect poorly on the company. So they're concerned about control. They want to make sure that they want to be comfortable. That you're not going to go out and do a deal or obligate them in some way or do something that's going to embarrass them. They want to feel that they are in control of this process and that you're not a loose cannon out there somewhere doing who knows what. So you want to have, I ask for three things. I ask for touch points. These touch points are daily touch points, usually with a client that I'm doing a lot of work with. In the case of Weiss, it's 7.15 a.m. phone calls. With another client, it may be twice a week phone calls or maybe just once a week. This is where we all get together on the phone and we say, okay guys, here's what's going on, here's where we're at, here's where we're dealing with. These are the challenges. Here's how the schedule looks going from here. Everything's great. Control. That has to do more with reporting and it's a two-way street. We provide our master's schedule to the client every week. The client provides us with the actual return reports that they're receiving from every campaign that they're running right now, every single day. In fact, as I've said before, I get one every four hours from Weiss. I get a return report every four hours that tells me what campaign, how much money came in, how many cancels, how we're looking. And then finally postmortems. This is a big part of control. The client needs to feel comfortable that when a campaign is done, when the dust is settled, that every possible lesson that can be learned from that campaign has been learned. And he needs to also be comfortable that those lessons will be incorporated into all future marketing plans. So I have postmortem meetings after major campaigns. Here's what we did right. Here's what we did wrong. Here's what we need to never do again. Here's what we always need to do from now on. And so this is big money right here and the client needs to feel that's being captured and acted on. And then what you'll be paid for each type of work. And we don't have time to go into all of this now. We've gone through it before. But here, some of the different ways that I'm paid by my various clients advances on projects. 50% when they reserve the time. The second half when the work is done. And this is simply a guarantee against royalties in most cases. Royalties apply to promotions where large numbers of the promotion will be mailed. But where the client's objective is not to make money. So obviously taking a percentage of profits would be idiotic of me. So for example, a direct mailing for a client. He's going to cold list. He wants to get new customers. His goal is to break even on that mailing and get a new customer. And so taking a percentage of profits would be stupid. So instead I take a percentage royalty on each piece mailed that comes to 5 cents per piece mailed. $50 per thousand. Commissions, this is when he is looking for a profit. You're doing a campaign for the client. He's looking to make a lot of money on that campaign. And a lot of times these are on fairly small lists so taking a royalty wouldn't make sense. So you take a percentage of sales. And of course that can be net sales, gross sales. However it works for you. You just adjust the percentage accordingly. Overrides. If you're building a big list but you're not working the back end somebody else is. You might ask for a small percentage on sales to customers. Look I'm busting my hump to bring in all these new customers for you. And then this other guy is over here creaming the file making all the money. That can't happen. So what I want is I want an override on your monthly sales to the customers that I bring in for you. These are all deals that I've done and that have made me a lot of money. Then there's also flat fees. There are retainers where you just, you know, for 10,000 a month you're guaranteed that you have for a shot at my time. Whatever the amount is you want to set. And then finally stock options are really good for when you're doing a lot of general consulting, partnering with a company that isn't quantifiable. I mean you're going in, the company calls you up and says, hey our PPC campaigns aren't working. No problem. Call this guy and go to this website and find out what your competitors best search terms are. And then give me a call back. Okay. You can't charge for that, right? And so, you know, you deal with that either with a retainer or with a stock option. A retainer would cover that. But also stock options are cool because the client doesn't have to pay them now. And essentially, you know, in three years you're going to go to him with your contract and you're going to say, okay I want, you know, I'm going to cash in my options now. And that can be a huge payday for you. I was going to say something else about the retainers. Oh yeah. If you're good at what you do, your client is going to ask you to be exclusive with him at some point. It's not a good idea unless there is a very substantial compensation package for you. And the reason for that is once you do that, you will have all your eggs in one basket. If there's a tsunami and the company is wiped out, if the owner gets hit by a bus, if, you know, a million terrible things happen, you're sitting there and this happened to me twice in my career with no client and with an overhead. And you never want to be in that position. So if a client wants you to be exclusive with them, demand some kind of exclusivity compensation that will cover you if and when that client's business goes away or your relationship goes away. I mean a client sells a business and the new owner no longer wants to work with you. That happened with me once. And I had built a company up, huge, literally quadrupled it. Another company came in and bought it, thought they knew marketing better than I did. I quit. They went bankrupt in six months. I had a golden parachute in place that if you guys pissed me off for any reason at all and I quit, you got to give me a lot of money. And so I was taken care of. Be sure to specify when you'll be paid and how you can monitor your compensation. If you're marketing on the web, it's easy. You just get access to the shopping cart. A good client will give you any reporting you want in this area. One of the best answers that you can give to any objection that you ask for in terms of your proposal is, but what if you get hit by a bus? A company owner will say, well, I'll never lie to you about your compensation. But what if you get hit by a bus? I'll never pay you late, but what if you get hit by a bus? I'll always pay you for your work product even if you're no longer working with the company. But what if you get hit by a bus? So it's a magic phrase. Use it. Make sure that you disclose in your proposal that this relationship will be Memorial I.S.O. legal agreement. Everyone's on board with that. Why? Because you might get hit by a bus. Determine the agreement. I like three years' cancellation clause. I like them to be 90 days' canceled by either party that gives me time to punt if I need to replace a client. And making it clear that I might walk with 90 days' notice also gives a client pause in terms of some of our dealing. And then use of your work product after cancellation. Look, just because we're not working together anymore doesn't mean you can use my copy and not pay me a royalty. Okay. And then finally, the last chapter of my proposals is the next step. Very short. Give the client a clear picture of what will happen next. We're going to meet and talk about this proposal, answer questions, make whatever adjustments are necessary. And then once that's done, we'll just send this proposal to the lawyer staff and draw up the legal agreement. Now, if you think through this, and I don't have a lot of time to wrap up, but if you think through this and you've done this the way that we've talked about here, you've already handled every objection, you've handled every major question the guy could have, and there's no reason for this not to be just a greasy skit straight in the lawyer's office. And go back through it, think about what we've talked about. I'm sure you'll see that that's the case. These are the errors to avoid. I think we promised five. I came up with eight. Number one, we've talked about lack of preparation. You don't start with a proposal. Proposal is the middle of the process. Do your homework. Don't make it a reading assignment. The big mistake, especially young writers, make, and also people who know that they're full of crap and are hoping I won't figure it out, the major mistake they make is that they feel that they need to make it eternal to be immortal. I don't need 20 pages to read. I need you to tell me what the vision is, what the challenges are, how we're going to overcome those challenges, how much you're going to charge me. If you can do that for me in four pages, you got me. If I got to read 30, I'll put it down next to the John, and the next time I'm in there, maybe I'll look at a few pages. Don't feel like you have to make it a reading assignment. I love this one. Pompous Language and Business School Chargent. Any contract or proposal that comes to me that says something like, we're going to facilitate a synergistic relationship is going straight in the trash. It might have been a great idea. It might have made me a billionaire, but I don't want to work with people who talk like that. To me, that's a dead giveaway that somebody read way too many business books, and he's been reading instead of working and actually producing. Plus, I just don't like people like that. Maybe it's a biker thing. Excess complexity, unnecessary detail. Again, unnecessary detail, just enough to get so that everyone's clear on what we're going to do. You can talk about, we're going to test price. Don't say we're going to test price at $39, $49, $59, and $69. Those are matters for meetings later on into which everybody will have input. It just gives people an opportunity to object. Failing to fully disclose your compensation. We talked about that. Do not be timid. It's like the old thing. The ugliest guy in the bar can get the most beautiful woman. If he thinks he can get the most beautiful woman, if he exudes confidence. The best looking guy in the bar won't be able to get her if he's a timid little mouse. It's the same thing here. You present your compensation with a straight face. You say, this is what I get. This is what I'm going to charge you. I have on many occasions with clients prior even to doing the proposal. One time, when I was writing the proposal, the purpose of this proposal is for you to pay me a million dollars in the next 12 months. That's my purpose in this proposal. How am I going to do that? I'm going to do that by making you $10 million in the next 12 months. Here's how I'm going to do it. Don't be shy about your fee. It undermines your credibility. It makes you look like a loser. Let them know right up front. I wouldn't be talking to you if I didn't believe that you're going to pay me a whole lot of money in the next 12 months. I think you're going to smile every time you sign a check because you're going to be making 10 times more than me. Blowing the deadline is a huge one. It ties into these other things here. When you put in too much detail, if you think it has to sound like something that some idiot professor at Harvard wrote, you're going to end up blowing the deadline. Your client is going to interpret that as being, first of all, that you're not really that excited about the project. Secondly, this is somebody that blows deadlines that I'm thinking about partnering with. Get it to them. Finally, typos. Make sure you get it proofread. You're a wordsmith for crying out loud. If your postures are in the wrong places and you have misspellings and grammatical errors all the way through, then you're going to have problems. Those are the eight fatal errors. We don't have many questions and we're out of time, so let me just go back and look real quick here. Cheryl wants to know how much detail do you just do the bullets or do you get into more details on what you'll be doing? Cheryl, a proposal really is more about the relationship because that's the thing that is about to get carved in stone. How we're going to get there, the general direction is also important. Our general mission, our general goals and acknowledgement of our challenges. Those things are all very important. There are things everyone can agree on. The nitty-gritty detail of precisely how we're going to move forward from here in terms of what products we're going to introduce first. That kind of thing is going to doom you because you're going to end up in long discussions about other ways to do it. Remember, what you want here is for this to go to an attorney and result in a contract. The things that are crucial are what kind of work I'm going to do for you and what you're going to pay me for it. Everything else that you have in that proposal is just a way to get everybody on the same page. Any quick copy advice for a left-brained thinker? Well, Cheryl, as a matter of fact, yeah. Everyone's going to think we set this up, but yeah, I do. Get Tony's new product, the ultimate desktop copycatch. I've never seen things like it. The thing is phenomenal. I wish I could say that I had anything to do with creating this thing. I did Tony went to school on my work, spent time talking to me about it over the last year, and has actually spent two years studying the copy that I've done, successful copy I've done online and offline. He's got almost 1,100 pages into huge bound volumes, or binders. He's got six getting started audios, three videos. The thing is amazing. The real amazing thing of it is that it's not just a bunch of tips. He goes into the process that I go through and that other top copywriters go through to solve problems or to write a lead or to write a fascination. It's all laid out in such exquisite detail. Plus, the way it's set up, let's say you're stuck for a headline, you just flip over to the page for headlines and problem solved. You've got more ideas than you could shake a stick at. I strongly suggest everyone look at that, especially since there's a 100% money back guarantee on it. You can get your money back if you don't like it. Alright guys, I hope this helped. Thanks for attending and I'll see you next time. Take care guys. Bye.
